top.value: • Amplified advocacy and representation in global climate fora: The CCFAH supports MCs in climate negotiations (e.g. UNFCCC COP30) and advocate for increased finance for climate resilience country priorities. • Integrated policy coherence across Rio Conventions: Through the implementation of the Commonwealth Living Lands Charter, technical assistance provided to align national strategies with UNFCCC, CBD, and UNCCD goals, including the use of Indigenous knowledge and nature-based solutions. • Gender-responsive and youth-inclusive climate action: - Operationalisation of the Commonwealth Collaborative Network on Gender-Responsive Climate Action and youth engagement platforms to ensure equitable participation in climate decision-making. - Negotiations training focused on youth engagement in global climate and environment resilience fora. • Knowledge generation and sharing: Development of publications, workshops, and training through the Commonwealth NDC Programme and other networks to build capacity and disseminate best practices.

An effective NHRI is a key mechanism of a national human rights protection system, helping to prevent human rights violations; offering redress where they occur; holding the government to account and ensuring that citizens are aware of their rights and national institutions of their obligations. 

Currently, just over half of Commonwealth member countries have NHRIs. Of those that do have NHRIs, a number are not fully compliant with the Paris Principles, the international gold standard of reference for this kind of institution. The Paris Principles establish benchmarks for independence and effectiveness of NHRIs. Pluralism is a key requirement of the Paris Principles, ensuring that equality concerns, including gender equality, are mainstreamed in the establishment, functions and work of NHRIs. 

The presence of a Paris Principle compliant NHRI is one of the indicators for the achievement of Sustainable Development Goal 16: Promote peaceful and inclusive societies for sustainable development, provide access to justice for all and build effective, accountable and inclusive institutions at all levels. The Commonwealth Strategic Plan 2017/2018-2021/2022 identifies Human Rights promoted and protected, and participation in the UN’s UPR process improved as an intermediate outcome under public institutions, including NHRIs as a key mechanism for the promotion and protection of human rights. 

The project aims to: 

1) Advocate with and support member states in the process of establishing an NHRI where one does not exist. This process takes many years (beyond one strategic plan period) so sustained engagement and mobilization of sustained political will, are core requirements;

2) Strengthen NHRIs that are not fully operationalized, fall short of the Paris Principles, or require empowerment in a strategic area of rights protection (For example violence against women and girls or equality and non-discrimination);

3) Facilitate exchanges of best practice and networking among NHRIs through the Commonwealth Forum of National Human Rights Institutions (CFNHRI), including on fulfilling their mandates with specific consideration to issues of gender equality and equality and non-discrimination.

An election conducted according to internationally accepted standards has become an important indicator of a country’s commitment to democracy and democratic processes. Moreover, there is now a common understanding of what constitutes a transparent, credible and inclusive election.

Despite a positive trajectory in the conduct and management of elections for several Commonwealth member states, challenges persist. This is evidenced by the fact that for a number of countries, even after a few election cycles, observer recommendations on key aspects of the electoral process are unchanged.  These recommendations fall into three categories, technical, political or legal/constitutional. The successful implementation of all three hinge on political will. 

In addition, there are emerging global trends/issues that have posed a challenge to the credibility of the electoral processes and of key stakeholders such as those relating to political party or campaign financing, the role of social media in elections and the use of technologies in elections which require closer scrutiny to ensure the measures used to address them are in line with international best practice. 

The purpose of this programme is to deploy international observers who lend credence to the electoral process, strengthen the capacity of key electoral institutions and stakeholders managing and/or engaged in the electoral process through a systematic and coordinated program of activities framed around the electoral cycle approach, as well as address the political dynamics of an election through integrated political initiatives (including through the Secretary-General’s Good Office).  

Strategically, at the highest level, this programme aims to contribute to Strategic Outcome 1: Greater Adherence to Commonwealth Political Values and Principles but more specifically at Intermediate Outcome 1.3: Member States Conduct Fair, Credible and Inclusive Elections. 

The programme seeks to achieve this aim through the following key initiatives: 

Deployment of Commonwealth election observer missions

Mobilise and strengthen mechanisms that promote and entrench good electoral practice as well as provides opportunities for follow-up of recommendation, conflict prevention and mitigation, and peer-to-peer support, through: 

Technical assistance support to EBMs

Engaging the Commonwealth Electoral Network, including through the development of knowledge products that contribute thought leadership on key electoral issues and promote and embed key norms and principles. 

Good Offices engagement, particularly as related to the pre-election, election and post-election periods, is an important component of the Secretariat's support to member countries.

As a 2022 CHOGM mandate, The Commonwealth Virtual Centre for Small States (VCSS) is intended to connect the Commonwealth Small States with the Secretariat and other accredited and partner organisations by means of a virtual platform for small states. The VCSS is meant to facilitate information and knowledge exchange as well as to access technical trainings based on toolkits developed by the Secretariat. In the medium term to encourage more joint collaboration of activities among the Commonwealth member Small States.

As part of the debt management capacity building programme
delivered by the Secretariat, the Debt Management Unit (DMU) develops and maintains the Secretariat's debt recording and management system. This software, first released on a modest scale in 1985, has grown over the years into an advanced system featuring a wide array of functions and a rich set of reports  allowing countries to comprehensively record, monitor and generate information on the debt situation for use by both local entities as well as international institutions.

The software is regularly maintained and enhanced to address
the technological gap in the system, emergence of new debt
management techniques, adoption of sound debt management
practices, new global debt initiatives, availability of more complex financing products, need for closer compliance with modern accounting principles and international recording and reporting standards and increasing expectations of member countries for the software to embrace state-of-the-art technologies.

In May 2012, the board of governors approved a budget of £3.4 million to be sourced from CFTC funds for the purposes of developing and implementing a new Secretariat debt management system (now known as Commonwealth Meridian). Further to the Board's decision, the Secretariat decided to outsource the development of the system and awarded a contract to Infosys Ltd (India based company) to redesign and develop the next state of the art debt management system to better support member countries. A fixed cost price contract was subsequently signed in September 2015. The contract was for a period of three (3) years originally planned to end August 2018 but later extended and completed in January 2019. The project period included a warranty period of one year.

Commonwealth Meridian is designed to effectively support modern debt management offices and includes a comprehensive set of debt management functions covering loan management, securities management, contingent liabilities management, lending management, grants management, financial derivatives, liability management operations, sub-national debt management, private sector external debt management, portfolio analysis and comprehensive reports based on internationally recognised standards. 

After a successful User Acceptance Testing and a Warranty period, during which the Commonwealth Meridian was piloted in 11 countries, the system is now available for live implementation in all member countries that use the Secretariat's debt management system. 

Implementation of Commonwealth Meridian in member states will be done in phases to efficiently manage the demand and expectations from member countries. The first phase (2019/20) will see the implementation of Commonwealth Meridian in the countries that took part in the pilot implementation stage and a few other priority countries. In total, the first phase will cover 17 countries from which 10 countries will be targeted to fully adopt Commonwealth Meridian at the end of the 2019/20 financial year.

In line with the 2019/20 delivery plan, the project aims to have 10 member countries fully adopting Commonwealth Meridian at the end of 2019/20 financial year and 25 member countries at the end of the 20/21 financial year.

In addition to Commonwealth Meridian implementation in the 17 countries, the project will undertake comprehensive capacity building activities to build a team of Commonwealth Meridian experts to complement the DMU team. This team of experts will be key to the implementation of Commonwealth Meridian in member states.

DMU has made a commitment to support CS-DRMS for the next three(3) years from 2019/20 finical year. DMU intends to implement Commonwealth Meridian in all the countries by June 2022 and phase out CS-DRMS.

At CHOGM 2018 Heads adopted the Declaration on the Commonwealth Connectivity Agenda. The Commonwealth Connectivity Agenda represents a new member-led initiative to boost intra-Commonwealth trade to US$2 trillion by 2030 and expand investment, by providing a platform for structured knowledge and experience sharing on hard and soft capacity, as undertaking capacity building around connectivity. The Commonwealth Connectivity Agenda represents the first connectivity framework spanning the globe involving such a diverse group of countries- all previous efforts have been regional. 

The Connectivity Agenda will support this goal by convening Senior Trade Officials and Trade Ministers Meetings to forge consensus on the parameters and content of these initiatives, and convening sector regulators, implementers, knowledge partners and the private sector under Clusters to operationalise these initiatives. Members will be guided by a Connectivity Agenda Action Plan which will be a living document containing the cluster activities that members themselves decide to pursue to reach the goal established by Heads. Oversight of the Connectivity Agenda will be by Commonwealth Trade Ministers and their Senior Trade Officials.

By conducting analysis of unfolding global trade dynamics and developments, including multilateral and regional trade negotiations, and undertaking advocacy activities this project aims to promote the effective participation of members, particularly the most capacity-constrained ones, namely small states, LDCs and Sub-Saharan African countries, in global trade and to enhance intra-Commonwealth trade and investment flows.

As demonstrated in our past policy research, e.g. the Commonwealth Trade Reviews 2015 and 2018, global trade is undergoing profound structural shifts. All governments are looking for options to enhance their trade performance and through it expand employment opportunities. This was also clearly expressed in the 2017 Commonwealth Trade Ministers’ Meeting. 

The global trading system is clearly facing challenging times: COVID-19 has caused a dramatic collapse in world growth, trade and investment; multilateral trade negotiations are virtually stalled with the Doha Round incomplete; there is a growing pervasiveness of protectionist measures, mostly recently in COVID-19-related medical supplies, with widespread discontent about globalisation and trade liberalisation; trade policy reversals of the USA and post-Brexit developments in Europe are causing heightened policy uncertainty. This is happening at a time when the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) call for more concerted action to use trade to help trigger greater growth, development and poverty reduction in the world’s poorest countries.

For many members, the emerging trade landscape is a challenging one where the certainties of previous decades are being eroded. This may imply that developing productive capacity to exploit trading opportunities will be severely constrained. Also, there remains much untapped potential to further leverage the ‘Commonwealth effect’ to grow trade flows and investment linkages between our members. 

As world trade is confronted with unprecedented challenges, the global support architecture available to small states, LDCs and other vulnerable developing countries has to adapt to the new realities. Under these circumstances, the Commonwealth’s role in providing thought-leadership on global trade issues and advocating for the trade-related development concerns of the smallest, poorest and most vulnerable countries cannot be overemphasised. The Commonwealth has always been a great force for global good in promoting a free, fair and inclusive global trading system and it is the moment for the Commonwealth - that has 31 small states, 13 LDCs and 19 sub-Saharan African countries as members - to gear up to play a catalytic role for making international trade an effective means of achieving development goals.    

Given the above, analytical, policy and advocacy work emanating from this project will contribute to:
(1) expanding the Commonwealth trade advantage and deepening intra-Commonwealth trade and investment cooperation; 
(2) improving multilateral and regional trade governance to assist members achieve meaningful trade deals;
(3) strengthening the role of trade in delivering SDGs; and
(4) securing development-friendly outcomes while addressing emerging trade issues.

The project has a strong focus on small states, LDCs and Sub-Saharan African members in promoting, among others, international consensus-building on trade and development issues, while protecting and promoting these countries’ interests. The modus operandi for the project activities will be a combination of research, policy and analytical work and  impactful advocacy exercises.

CFAMM: Foreign Affairs Ministers Meeting to collectively address global issues such as climate change, economic instability, and threats to democracy, working towards a more resilient and sustainable future for all member countries. CFAMM typically takes place in New York in the margins of the United Nations General Assembly in September. This budget cycle, the meeting is projected to take place in March 2026 in London. CMAG: Support to convening the Ministerial Action Group and strengthening its capacity to proactively engage with member countries, promoting adherence to democratic principles and facilitating inclusive political dialogue and governance.

CMAG acts as the custodian of the Commonwealth’s fundamental political values. It is a high-level ministerial mechanism through which serious or persistent violations of these political values are addressed. It strives to encourage countries that fail to adhere to Commonwealth fundamental values to address these failures in a timely manner. 

The work of the Group complements the political initiatives of the Secretary-General, including her Good Offices.

CMAG has the ability to suspend a country from Commonwealth membership in the event of serious or persistent violations of Commonwealth fundamental political values. The option of suspension has been most commonly exercised in instances of an unconstitutional overthrow of a democratic government. However, that and other sanctions are available for other serious or persistent violations. CMAG is also able to indicate its concern over a country situation by placing that country on its formal agenda. This allows for a close degree of ministerial scrutiny of developments of concern in that country.

The aim of CMAG's work is to encourage member countries to adhere more closely to Commonwealth fundamental political values.

CMAG is convened by the Commonwealth Secretary-General. CMAG meetings are facilitated by the Secretariat and the Group is provided with comprehensive briefings.

1. Evidence informing the approach The approach is informed by documented gaps in Commonwealth member state AI and digital governance maturity, including uneven national AI readiness, fragmented data ecosystems, and limited institutional frameworks — particularly in SIDS, LDCs, and small states. Internally, the Secretariat's own assessment identified the absence of a formally operationalised AI governance architecture as a material risk to both credibility and programme delivery quality. The dual-track model adopted by SA6 draws on established enterprise architecture principles — specifically the separation of governance authority from delivery execution, consistent with TOGAF's distinction between architecture governance and solution delivery. This prevents two well-documented failure modes: fragmented, uncoordinated technology adoption driven by local experimentation; and governance retrofitted reactively after deployment rather than embedded upfront. Evidence from comparable international organisations and Commonwealth programme cycles confirms that governance-first approaches — where standards, risk classification, and ethical conformity requirements are established before scale-up — produce more durable institutional outcomes than capability-led models. 2. Contribution to programme-level impact SA6 operates as an enterprise-wide enabling function rather than a standalone delivery programme. It creates the institutional conditions under which all three resilience pillars — Democratic, Economic, and Environmental — can be delivered with greater rigour, efficiency, and accountability. Specifically: governance frameworks and digital tools strengthening democratic processes and civic participation; data quality and analytics infrastructure supporting evidence-based economic policy and trade facilitation; and geospatial and early warning platforms enabling climate resilience and disaster response. The dual-track model ensures this contribution is both internal — through AI governance architecture, workforce capability, and data governance standards — and external, through the CAIC partnership ecosystem and structured capacity-building delivery to member states. Both tracks report through a single governance and assurance model, ensuring coherence across the full results chain through to the Strategic Results Framework. 3. COMSEC's comparative advantage The Secretariat's comparative advantage in delivering SA6 lies in three mutually reinforcing institutional characteristics that no other actor can replicate at Commonwealth scale. First, its position as a trusted, politically neutral convener across 56 member states enables it to broker governance standards and technical partnerships that bilateral or commercial actors cannot credibly facilitate. This is particularly significant in sensitive domains such as electoral integrity, data sovereignty, and AI regulatory alignment. Second, the Secretariat's established governance architecture — including the AI Governance Framework aligned to ISO/IEC 42001, the AI Risk Register, and the mandatory ethical conformity assessment process — provides a credible, standards-based assurance model that partners and member states can rely on. This governance infrastructure, built and owned internally, is a tangible institutional asset that differentiates the Secretariat from looser coordination mechanisms. Third, the Secretariat's network reach — combining its 56-country membership, Commonwealth Accredited Organisations, FCDO and development finance relationships, and private sector partnerships under the CAIC — gives it a resource mobilisation and partnership brokering capacity that is structurally unavailable to single-country donors or commercial providers. SA6 deploys all three advantages in service of responsible digital transformation, acting as enabler, convener, and trusted marketplace curator rather than developer or direct implementer.

This document illustrates a 4-year work plan for the financial years 2017/18 – 2021/22 that will help achieve intermediate outcome of the Commonwealth Secretariat strategic plan: Young people engaged and empowered to meaningfully participate in political and development processes.  It will result in young people across the Commonwealth being empowered - socially, politically and economically - to ensure equitable development outcomes, and their optimal contribution to development and democracy.  
Young people constitute one quarter of the world’s population, and one third of the population in developing nations. This signals a vibrant and hopeful resource for the world. But they are more than numbers. Their struggles for social justice and equality are increasingly visible and articulate. They have demonstrated their progressive vision for the world in multiple ways, be it as citizens, voters, or in organised youth movements. They have the greatest stake in equitable and sustainable development, and are also well-positioned to contribute meaningfully to this. 
At the same time, we are witnessing global and national moves that work against young people’s vision for themselves and the world. Young people are reported to be poorer at every stage of their life now, than their parents. In a context of rising inequality and diminishing social support systems, young people form a large proportion of the world’s unemployed, they have challenges accessing affordable education, and basic services. Mainstreaming youth interests and capabilities are becoming even more critical in this context. 
This project proposes a multi-pronged approach to addressing these broad social, political and economic challenges, focused on: 
the role of the Youth Sector in delivering youth empowerment strategies, and 
the ability of all development sector players across sectors, including young people themselves, to deliver holistic and equitable development outcomes for youth and (directly and indirectly) for society. 

The three core approaches to this multi-faceted strategy are the strengthening of:  

Policy and Institutions: Policy change and institutional reform in order to deliver for young people, with them; 
Capacity: Strengthening stakeholder capacity to inform changes in policies and institutions both within and without the youth sector;   
Connections: Creating collaborative environments that bring governments, young people, civil society and all other development sectors together to ensure the realisation of young people’s rights, and to provide platforms for their contribution to development and democracy. Mechanisms that support young people’s participation in global and national governance are a critical part of this. 
The approaches above will be supported by the evidence base of recent evaluation and baseline reports, and the Commonwealth’s existing research and policy guidance tools developed in collaboration with member states and all stakeholders. 
Commonwealth Youth Programme Evaluation Report (2017)
2016 Youth Development Index (YDI)
Youth Entrepreneurship Policy Guide (2015)
Youth Mainstreaming Handbook (2017)
Youth Work Baseline Report (2016)
Guide to Optimising National Youth Development Systems (2017)
Draft to Establishing and Strengthening National Youth Councils (2017)
State of Student Governance in the Commonwealth (2016)

Strategies will focus on the most effective and sustainable impact pathways for policy and practice changes in government and other stakeholders that offer value for money and practical, effective approaches to changing mindsets, institutions and systems/mechanisms and ultimately bring change for young people. Strategies will include policy influencing through:  

Supporting governments to implement youth-mainstreamed and evidence-based approaches to development planning for youth in order to ensure youth empowerment and equitable development outcomes for youth
Supporting governments to formulate and implement youth entrepreneurship strategies in order to ensure a sustainable and supported ecosystem for youth entrepreneurship.  
Supporting the development of youth networks and organisations at national and global level to ensure young people’s participation in global and national governance, and policy and practice transformation on youth issues and broader societal issues (Eg: the Commonwealth Youth Council, the Commonwealth Students’ Association and the Commonwealth Alliance of Young Entrepreneurs etc.),  
The development of policy and practice guidance, and education and training, for professionalisation of the youth work sector,
Supporting the establishment and strengthening of professional associations of youth workers in member countries in order to ensure the quality and integrity of the profession, and the strengthening of the global Commonwealth Alliance of Youth Workers’ Associations (CAYWA) in order to consolidate a global identity for youth workers, including for sharing knowledge and good practice and advocating for the profession at international level.

This project will utilise the Commonwealth’s comparative advantages and recognised global leadership position in youth development, including building on its long history of working with youth-led and youth-serving institutions, to advance the meaningful engagement of young people in policy and high-level governance processes.  

At the end of this four-year Commonwealth Youth Programme Project we will expect to see:
an increase in the number of youth-related policies such as standalone youth policies, youth work policy, youth entrepreneurship policy, policies that ensure youth participation in governance (both political and institutional governance) including within higher secondary and tertiary educational institutions;
enhanced coordinated, youth mainstreamed approaches to delivering outcomes for young people, including a youth lens in national and global planning in all economic, social and political spheres and in all sectors. 
strong national, regional and pan-Commonwealth youth networks that are contributing to policy setting, and leading work to deliver the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG).
National Youth Development Index (YDI) and other youth-related research that help track long-term outcomes for youth and enable evidence-based planning for young people in Commonwealth member states.
The recognition of youth work (the key profession specializing in youth engagement) as a professional category in Commonwealth member states.

This programme seeks to improve access to climate finance for small and other vulnerable Commonwealth States through a combination of direct long-term technical assistance; evidence based research; and advocacy to influence the international climate finance architecture. This will be delivered through a number of mutually reinforcing activities where the Commonwealth Secretariat can add particular value to national, regional and international initiatives. The programme is expected to improve and strengthen the climate resilience of these countries.

The key delivery mechanisms are as follows: 

  
The Climate Finance Access Hub (CFAH): The CFAH’s innovative approach provides long-term programmatic support, building on-the-ground capacity, as compared to other climate readiness initiatives offering financial and short-term advisory support, and fills a clear niche in a crowded landscape. The strong demand from member states for the CFAH services (20 countries have requested assistance) further emphasises the need for such an initiative. 

The CFAH operates from Mauritius with strategic, technical, operational and oversight support from London.

2.	Research, information gathering and advocacy on climate finance: The programme will also develop and advance its research portfolio on climate finance. With a unique and growing programme, there are opportunities to embed innovative lesson learning mechanisms in the CFAH to advance our research portfolio and address knowledge gaps in the sector. The research topics will include country case studies focussing on the climate finance contexts of small and vulnerable studies, and other pertaining climate finance issues that can advance international climate finance negotiations and bridge knowledge gaps. 

The research will also underpin the Commonwealth’s advocacy strategy on climate finance. 

The successful implementation of the programme will strengthen the institutional capacity of key ministries and agencies mandated to attract and manage climate finance in small and other vulnerable states; support the development of a pipeline of climate change projects in these countries; increase the evidence base on climate finance, with particular focus on small and vulnerable states; and advance the Secretariat’s climate finance advocacy. 

The programme will assist member states to realise the targets set out in the Paris Climate Change Agreement and catalyse the attainment of the 2030 sustainable development goals (SDGs).

The Commonwealth countries (developing, emerging economies, least developed countries and small and vulnerable economies including island states) experience a wide range of challenges in improving their trade competitiveness which translate to low shares in global trade. Small domestic markets and limited production capacities make many of these countries dependent on global imports. This increases their vulnerability and lead to limited employment opportunities and wide spread poverty, making them susceptible to dangers of food shortages. In order to pay for their global imports, the exports of these countries need to be increased to enable them build their foreign exchange reserves, increase their employment opportunities, especially for women and youth and generate incomes for their poor. 

 The COVID 19 pandemic has disrupted the economic development gains made by many of these countries post 2008 global crisis. Re building global competitiveness post COVID 19 will rely on the ability of governments and businesses to respond to the crisis by effectively implementing policies, plans and strategies to improve productivity and raise living standards in a stable, market-friendly environment to achieve inclusive development

Many of the Commonwealth countries need support and technical assistance to rebuild their trade competitiveness. This support is needed to diversify their export baskets and export destinations; improve their market access through well negotiated trade agreements; build trade competitiveness within their services sectors through regulatory cooperation and reforms; facilitate investments in areas where limited supply capacities exist; develop comprehensive national trade policies; and improve their trade facilitation processes in order to lower their cost of trading. The Commonwealth Trade Competitiveness Program aims at providing technical support in these five areas in a holistic fashion so that any country receiving this support is fully enabled to effectively participate in global trade in a manner that leads to generation of additional employment, especially for women and youth, and prosperity for the country.    

The program has in the past three years (since 2017) received formal requests from the Trade Ministries for trade competitiveness support in the above areas from 22 countries (Barbados, Belize, Botswana, Brunei, Cameroon, Gambia, Grenada, India, Jamaica, Kenya, Lesotho, Malawi, Mauritius, Mozambique, Nigeria, Papua New Guinea, Seychelles, Sierra Leone, Solomon Islands, South Africa, Sri Lanka, Tonga),  facilitated 3 regional projects (East African Community (EAC),  Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) and CARICOM/CAIPA, and supported 2 Pan Commonwealth projects- the Intra- Commonwealth SME Association and the Annual Forum for developing countries program in collaboration with the Institute for Sustainable Development and UNCTAD.  (Annex 8- some example of formal requests from these countries). 

The global COVID 19 pandemic slowed down the deployment and completion of some of the programs initiated in FY 2019/20. Contracts that had been issued to undertake work in Belize were cancelled as a result of the restriction on travel and previously scheduled stakeholder consultations were put on hold. The team in response in partnership with the government institutions has deployed most of these activities using virtual platforms. However some scheduled projects will be carried on to the 2020/21 FY as these could not be delivered virtually. 

Based on the formal requests received from the Trade Ministries of the member states and the regional bloc, the trade competitiveness program provides technical assistance to the member countries. 32 out of 54 Commonwealth countries are natural resources and commodity-dependent countries exporting commodities like metals and minerals and few agricultural commodities (like cotton, tea, etc.).
 
The Covid-19 pandemic is affecting global trade in three main areas: demand disruption, supply disruption and workforce impacts. Over two-thirds of world trade occurs through global value chains (GVCs). According to the International Trade Centre, the disruption of three major GVC hubs – China, the EU and the USA – could result in a US$228 billion decrease in exports across GVCs. The pandemic has also caused a severe decline in commodity prices with its adverse consequences for the balance of trade and debt repayments, which will be quite significant for Commonwealth member countries that depend on commodity exports. The impact of the pandemic on the services sectors of travel, tourism and hospitality also has grave consequences for many countries, especially least developed countries and small island developing states.

Along with fiscal and monetary policies, reinvigorating global trade will be a key component of economic recovery after the pandemic and supporting the rebuilding of member’s trade competitiveness will be a critical step in the recovery process. 

The Trade Competitiveness Program delivers five outputs- Output 1- Export Diversification Program identifies New Products and New Markets for diversifying exports of the member states. It designs product-market specific export strategies. It builds negotiating capacity and provide technical assistance in trade negotiations to the countries to improve their global market access.  This then feeds into the Output 2- National Trade Policy Program where domestic constraints are identified which hinder a country from availing global trade opportunities and restricts the productive capacity of the producers. Given the growing importance of e-commerce and digital trade, an E commerce strategy becomes an important part of the National Trade Policies designed for the member states. Many member states have limited manufacturing capacities thereby making it important to add services in their export baskets. Identification of competitive services and developing a Services Export Strategy along with enhancing regulatory cooperation and reform to boost trade in services is delivered by the Output 3 -Trade in Services Program. In many areas, the member states enjoy trade competitiveness but lack supply capacities due to lack of investments. Output 4- The Investment Facilitation program identifies the potential investment areas and help in developing investment policies and plans for the member states. However, without trade facilitation measures in place cost of trading will remain high adversely impacting on trade competitiveness. Output 5- The Trade Facilitation program support member countries and/ regions with niche, highly targeted interventions that support their institutional capacity to reform their processes and implement trade facilitation initiatives. Pan Commonwealth programs that provide linkages for MSMEs is delivered through the Intra- Commonwealth SME Association.
 
The Secretariat is best placed as an organisation to provide this comprehensive support to the member countries. The Trade Competitiveness Section has developed a unique methodology to identify New Products and New Markets for export diversification. While other organisations recommend export diversification to the countries, the Secretariat is the only organisation which is able to provide technical support on How to Diversify Exports. Export diversification program is therefore a niche area for the Secretariat. Given its honest brokerage image many countries approach the Secretariat for technical assistance in their trade negotiations. No other multilateral organisations provide a country with such a technical assistance where the country is directly assisted in its trade negotiations. Most of the organisations like UNCTAD undertake research on WTO negotiating issues/ regional; trade issues and provide global trade policy advocacy. The Secretariat has also proved its expertise and a competitive edge over other organisations in terms of providing technical assistance through its other programs. Countries who had received support from other organisations like ITC for developing their national trade policies/export strategies have approached the Secretariat for their subsequent trade policies e.g., Jamaica. The trade in services program of the Secretariat has been hailed as a success by many small countries including Belize and Barbados. The Trade Ministers in the recent Trade Ministers meeting organised by the Secretariat in March 2017, expressed their appreciation of the Trade Competitiveness Program and have recommended further support to the program. Ministers also expressed their need for technical assistance in implementation of Trade Facilitation Agreement (TFA), which is first of its kind program initiated by the Secretariat where the Secretariat conducts cost of implementation of TFA for the countries and helps them in implementing as well as raising resources for the implementation of TFA.

This document illustrates a 3-year work plan for the financial years 2018/19 – 2021/22 that will help achieve intermediate outcome 3.0 of the Commonwealth Secretariat strategic plan: Strengthened sustainable policies reduce disparities and improve educational outcomes.  It will result in enhanced education and learning for children, adolescents, young people and adults across the Commonwealth – to fulfil their potential with dignity and equality in a healthy environment. 

The Commonwealth’s 54 member countries have a combined population of more than two billion, of which more than 60% are under 30 years of age. Yet, millions of school aged children and adolescents are out of school, whereas millions of young people and adults are unemployed because of skills mismatch in the labour market, indicating that the education system is a significant contributor to the unemployment crisis and is ineffective as it fails to prepare for life and unlock growth in the economy. In general, 15 to 17-year-olds are four times as likely to be out of school as are children aged 6 to 11. This is partly because primary and lower secondary education are compulsory in nearly every country, while upper secondary school is not. At the same time, these youths are often of legal working age. Many have no choice but to work while others try to combine going to school with employment. 
Girls are more likely than boys to never set foot in a classroom, despite efforts made and progress achieved over the past two decades. According to UIS data, 15 million girls of primary school age will never get the chance to learn to read or write in primary school compared to about 10 million boys. 
Education systems, including higher education systems have yet to fuel creativity and competitiveness for global markets, and to give the Commonwealth an edge in entrepreneurship and innovation. 

In line with the Education 2030 Framework for Action, this project proposes to enhance the quality of education and learning by focusing on: 
the capacity of the education sector in delivering fair and inclusive education and learning, making the benefits of education and learning available to all citizens of the Commonwealth, including through relevant content and learner centered approaches. 
the ability of all education sector players including learners themselves, to adapt, innovate, deliver or demonstrate learning outcomes for well-being and socio-economic development. Children, youth and adults gain the knowledge, skills, attitudes, behaviors and values they need to engage positively with society, make healthy choices, transition from secondary to higher education, or from school to work, and pursue multiple pathways to a productive and safe future.

To achieve the above, the project will focus more specifically on five areas of development 
Learners’ empowerment for active and responsible citizenship
Competent and well qualified educators using learner centered approaches and active pedagogies; educators trained in school leadership and management
Content design for relevant curricular content and learning resources
Education systems’ strengthening through policy, planning and management
Partnerships and collaborative processes

Member states will receive technical support to develop education policies and implementation mechanisms that create the competencies and learning achievements required for their citizens to prosper in the future; as well as to strengthen their education systems in order to respond to new economic, environmental, societal and individual needs and challenges. 

At the end of this three-year project we will expect to see:  
An increase in the number of effective education policies and practices for better quality education and learning across key sub-sectors and learning environments including non-formal education.
A strong and sustainable learning and knowledge network through the provision of platforms, tools and advisory services to embed policy and practice models for effective education systems.
A strengthened education sector workforce with professional, leadership and teaching skills contributing to robust education systems across member states.
A wider and more diverse group of organizations and partners supporting and contributing to the education project.

The Secretariat will work in partnership with the Commonwealth of Learning, the Association of Commonwealth Universities, the accredited organisations of the Commonwealth and other key partners, including regional bodies and UN agencies, to leverage the skills, resources, assets, network and authority that each partner brings to the table, as well as its complementarity.

This project enables the Commonwealth Secretariat to work with member countries to define and understand the phenomenon of violent extremism as it relates to their specific context, and then to strategise, plan and act accordingly to prevent and counteract its growth and counter its effects.

The CVE Unit’s programme of works covers four different areas with the aim to strengthen mechanisms to counter violent extremism in member countries and ultimately to encourage strong democracy, rule of law, the promotion and protection of human rights and respect for diversity.

In-depth technical assistance to 4 countries to improve their understanding of national CVE gaps and strengths, and to help implement effective policy and programming. *
Support to civil society organisations to strengthen their CVE capacity and their networks with the aim to encourage positive CSO-Government collaboration and build resilient communities.
Capacity building & awareness raising activities & workshops to improve member country’s ability to deal with violent extremism in all forms.
Research, communications, and campaigns to advance Pan Commonwealth understanding of CVE.
* One targeted country was chosen from each of four Commonwealth regions  through consultation with the Unit’s Steering Committee and their identified need for assistance in CVE aligned with the Commonwealth’s capacity to provide such assistance.

The Commonwealth is uniquely placed to assist member countries in sharing good practice and harnessing the role of governments and individuals to enhance the resilience of society to violent extremist messages. The Commonwealth advantage lies within it’s decades of experience in supporting governments and its shared values, cultural and regional diversity. Successful CVE strategies and programming involve not only national government officials but local government, youth, CSOs and grassroots organizations. The Commonwealth is unique in its ability to convene these parties and hold positive influence with all sections of society.

As part of the debt management capacity building programme
delivered by the Secretariat, the Debt Management Unit (DMU) develops and maintains the Secretariat's debt recording and management system. This software, first released on a modest scale in 1985, has grown over the years into an advanced system featuring a wide array of functions and a rich set of reports  allowing countries to comprehensively record, monitor and generate information on the debt situation for use by both local entities as well as international institutions.

The software is regularly maintained and enhanced to address
the technological gap in the system, emergence of new debt
management techniques, adoption of sound debt management
practices, new global debt initiatives, availability of more complex financing products, need for closer compliance with modern accounting principles and international recording and reporting standards and increasing expectations of member countries for the software to embrace state-of-the-art technologies.

In May 2012, the board of governors approved a budget of £3.4 million to be sourced from CFTC funds for the purposes of developing and implementing a new Secretariat debt management system (now known as Commonwealth Meridian). Further to the Board's decision, the Secretariat decided to outsource the development of the system and awarded a contract to Infosys Ltd (India based company) to redesign and develop the next state of the art debt management system to better support member countries. A fixed cost price contract was subsequently signed in September 2015. The contract was for a period of three (3) years originally planned to end August 2018 but later extended and completed in January 2019. The project period included a warranty period of one year.

Commonwealth Meridian is designed to effectively support modern debt management offices and includes a comprehensive set of debt management functions covering loan management, securities management, contingent liabilities management, lending management, grants management, financial derivatives, liability management operations, sub-national debt management, private sector external debt management, portfolio analysis and comprehensive reports based on internationally recognised standards. 

After a successful User Acceptance Testing and a Warranty period, during which the Commonwealth Meridian was piloted in 11 countries, the system is now available for live implementation in all member countries that use the Secretariat's debt management system. 

Implementation of Commonwealth Meridian in member states will be done in phases to efficiently manage the demand and expectations from member countries. The first phase (2019/20) will see the implementation of Commonwealth Meridian in the countries that took part in the pilot implementation stage and a few other priority countries. In total, the first phase will cover 17 countries from which 10 countries will be targeted to fully adopt Commonwealth Meridian at the end of the 2019/20 financial year.

In line with the 2019/20 delivery plan, the project aims to have 10 member countries fully adopting Commonwealth Meridian at the end of 2019/20 financial year and 25 member countries at the end of the 20/21 financial year.

In addition to Commonwealth Meridian implementation in the 17 countries, the project will undertake comprehensive capacity building activities to build a team of Commonwealth Meridian experts to complement the DMU team. This team of experts will be key to the implementation of Commonwealth Meridian in member states.

DMU has made a commitment to support CS-DRMS for the next three(3) years from 2019/20 finical year. DMU intends to implement Commonwealth Meridian in all the countries by June 2022 and phase out CS-DRMS.

The Commonwealth Election Professionals (CEP) Initiative Phase II* is an EBR-funded project designed to address the identified training needs of more junior staff members of Commonwealth Election Management Bodies (EMBs). In doing so, the project draws heavily on the experience and lessons learned from implementation of JEP Phase I, and aims to consolidate and extend impact achieved to date.

Operating under the auspices of the Commonwealth Electoral Network (CEN), CEP Phase II forms an integral part of the Secretariat’s offer to Commonwealth EMBs, promoting the delivery of more transparent, credible and inclusive elections. 

The proposal maps out a three-year project (1 March 2017 to 28 February 2020) at a total investment cost of GBP 850,000. The project includes the provision of two dedicated Secretariat staff, and is fully-funded by the Australian Department for Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT).

CEP Phase II addresses two core problems:
(i) the lack of professional development opportunities provided to more junior election professionals employed by permanent Commonwealth election management bodies (EMBs), and
(ii) the lack of capacity-building and training provided to permanent and temporary staff within micro and non-permanent EMBS in the Caribbean and Pacific regions. 

CEP Phase II will address these issues through:
(i) the design and delivery of a series of eight capacity-building events for a minimum of 110 election officials from a minimum of 40 Commonwealth EMBs;
(ii) the creation and moderation of an online community of interest for a minimum of 110 project participants on Commonwealth Connects for the purposes of peer support, experience and knowledge sharing, and
(iii) the provision of a series of three 9 to 12 month secondments for outstanding alumni from JEP Phase I to contribute to the work of the Commonwealth Electoral Network (CEN).

By project end,
(i) Participating election professionals will perform better in their roles, leading to assignment of new responsibilities and/or career progression within their EMB or civil service, and
(ii) EMBs will have reviewed electoral practices, procedures and policies and formulate recommendations to increase the fairness, credibility and inclusiveness of elections within their jurisdictions.

In equipping Commonwealth EMBs with a pool of expertise and specialist knowledge and supporting online network, the project will enhance the capacity of Commonwealth members to conduct fair, credible and inclusive elections (contributing to Intermediate Outcome 1.3 within Strategic Outcome 1, Democracy: Greater adherence to Commonwealth political values and principles, Commonwealth Strategic Plan 2017 – 2021.

February 2021 Update: In line with the related Grant Agreement with Australia, the second phase of the CEP Initiative (CEP Phase II) was originally due to end on 30 June 2020. As a result of the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on project implementation, CEP Phase II was extended in April 2020 until 31 March 2021 through a Deed of Amendment. 

Concurrently and at Australia’s request, the Secretariat entered into negotiations for a third phase project phase (CEP Phase III). Signed on 6 January 2021, the CEP Phase III Grant Agreement provides for a total EBR contribution of GBP900,000 for the period 1 January 2021 to 30 June 2025.

As a consequence of the new funding arrangement, the effective project end-date of CEP Phase II was brought forward to 31 December 2020. In consultation with SPPD and ahead of the adoption of the new Secretariat Strategic Plan, guidance was received to revise and extend the existing CEP Phase II PDD on PMIS .

CEP Phase III
As noted, the Australian EBR contribution provides for a total of GBP 900,000 for the period 1 January 2021 to 30 June 2025. Similarly to CEP Phase II, CEP Phase III includes provision for two dedicated project staff: the CEP Programme Officer and Assistant.

The strategic goal of this Project is to enhance the capacity of Commonwealth member countries to conduct fair, credible and inclusive electoral processes (contributing to Intermediate Outcome 1.3 within Strategic Outcome 1, Democracy: Greater adherence to Commonwealth political values and principles, Commonwealth Strategic Plan 2017/18 – 2020/21) by equipping Commonwealth EMBs with a pool of expertise and specialist knowledge and supporting online network.

By the end of the Project, the following intermediate outcomes will have been achieved:

*Participating election professionals are trained in international best practice as it relates to electoral management, and apply the knowledge and skills gained to their work within their Election Management Body (‘EMB’) or civil service.

*Participating election professionals perform better in their roles, leading to assignment of new responsibilities and/or career progression within their EMB or civil service.

*Participating election professionals review their EMB’s electoral practices, procedures and policies and formulate recommendations to increase the fairness, credibility, and inclusiveness of electoral processes.

It is noted that CEP Phase III includes measures to mitigate the impact of the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic on project implementation, including the holding of virtual meetings in addition to in-person training events.

Many Commonwealth member countries lack the legal; taxation and revenue management;  environmental policies and systems to ensure that natural resource wealth leads to sustainable economic and social development.  These issues will be exacerbated by the covid-19 pandemic.
How the problem will be addressed:
This Project will provide direct technical assistance to 13 Commonwealth Member countries (The Bahamas, Barbados, Belize, Botswana, Cook Islands, Ghana, Guyana, Jamaica, Kiribati, Namibia, Papua New Guinea, Swaziland, and Vanuatu), to assist them better regulate and tax the natural resources sector. It will also assist these governments manage the revenue from natural resources. 
Outputs: Over the next four years this Project will at a minimum:
draft at least 5 laws, which if enacted will lead to the superior regulation of significant and transformative economic activity
design at least 4 new fiscal/tax regimes, which if implemented, will lead to billions of dollars of additional government revenue;
draft at least 3 new environmental policies and regulations, which if implemented, will lead to improved environmental protection;
design at least 2 sovereign wealth funds to improve the management of natural resource revenues.
Value Added of the Commonwealth Secretariat's Oceans and Natural Resources Advisory Division ‘(ONR’): ONR is uniquely placed to implement this Project because of its existing relationship as trusted adviser to member countries, and the methodology of our approach:
Demand Led. All outputs that are produced by this Project are demanded by Commonwealth member countries. This Project will never produce an output which isn't asked for by a country. This contrasts to other technical assistance projects which can be a difficult compromise between the priorities of the donor, the demands of the recipient and a consultancy company's desire to produce work they can charge for.
Legal Drafting. This Project does not just offer policy advice. It also assists countries implement this advice by drafting laws, regulations and contracts. This is special. Most consultancy companies and international organisations that offer policy advice do not have legal drafting capability and do not undertake such work. The advice ONR offers is thus well-rounded and much more likely to be implemented and result in concrete outcomes. In addition, ONR is the only organisation to offer countries technical assistance on deep sea mining.
Expertise of the Team. ONR has a highly experienced team which draws expertise from the private sector, international organisations and government. Its expertise also cuts across environmental management, legal form and economics.
